subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data are organised into tables and graphs with headings and units, showing reasonable clarity
Solid algebraic derivation confirming the T â M^(â1/2) relationship
Validation of the simulatorâs 365âday orbital period against the theoretical formula demonstrates care
weaknesses: No quantitative treatment or discussion of uncertainties or error propagation
Inconsistencies in decimal notation and omitted intermediate calculation steps reduce transparency
Regression details (equation, RÂ²) and uncertainty estimates are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall falls in the 1â2 band but closer to the lower end: communication is somewhat clear but lacks precision and any real consideration of uncertainties or statistical rigour, warranting 1 mark under the totalâ5 constraint.

strengths: Clear and logical theoretical derivation from Newtonâs law to TÂ²=4ÏÂ²rÂ³/GM
Wellâdefined variables table distinguishing dependent, independent and controlled variables
Research question is explicitly linked to Newtonian gravitation with sufficient astronomical background
weaknesses: Contextualisation remains broad and textbookâlevel, lacking reference to current observational data or scientific debate
Methodology description omits essential operational details (number of trials, measurement protocol) hindering reproducibility
No explanation of how the simulatorâs elliptical orbits were handled
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Placed at the top of the 1â2 band: the research question is clear and logically motivated, and theoretical understanding is strong, but contextual depth and methodological detail are insufficient for higher marks. Given the need to scale to a total of 5, this criterion receives 2 marks.

strengths: Conclusion restates how varying solar mass influences orbital period, directly addressing the research question
Succinct linkage between processed data and the conclusion
weaknesses: Comparison to actual planetary periods is superficial, lacking quantitative tables or scholarly references
No discussion of how neglected factors (eccentricity, measurement error) affect the conclusionâs robustness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Fits the lower end of the 1â2 band: conclusion is stated and roughly consistent with analysis but lacks depth in scientific context and justification. Allocated 1 mark to achieve the overall total of 5.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (simulator validation, omission of Keplerâs laws) showing critical reflection
Recognition of orbital ellipticity at higher masses indicates awareness of simulator limitations
weaknesses: Suggested improvements are realistic but lack detail on implementation steps and impact on accuracy
Does not explain the relative magnitude of identified weaknesses on the final results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Corresponds to the 1â2 band: specific weaknesses are described, but improvement suggestions are brief and undeveloped. Allocated 1 mark so that all criteria sum to 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student clearly states the research question and links it to Newtonian gravitation and orbital period, giving enough astronomical background (values of solar mass, EarthâSun distance, concept of year) for the reader to understand why the variable is worth studying. However, the contextualisation remains largely textbook-level and personal (âme ha fascinadoâ), with little reference to current scientific debate or real observational data. This fulfils the descriptor of a broad but not exceptionally specific or specialised context, so the mid-band (3) in 3-4 is most appropriate.

Uncertainties are never calculated or discussed; there is no treatment of error propagation, simulator resolution, or theoretical assumptions. The only hint is the authorâs qualitative remark that results may not be accurate. This is limited evidence, thus band 1-2 with the minimum score.

Methodological considerations are stated only superficially: a variables table and a short comment that r and mEarth are controlled. There is no discussion of ensuring a circular orbit, the effect of numerical precision in the simulator, or how many data points are needed for reliability. Consequently, the considerations are present but minimal, satisfying the lower end of the 1-2 band.

The evaluation section identifies two specific methodological weaknesses: reliance on simulator data without external validation and the failure to use Keplerâs laws once non-circular orbits appear. These weaknesses are described but the magnitude of their impact on results is only implied, not fully analysed, matching the âdescribes specific weaknessesâ level (band 3-4) at score 3.

The methodology is described in general terms (use PhET âGravity and Orbits,â vary the Star Mass slider, record the orbital period, keep r constant), but crucial operational details are missing or later abandoned. No step-by-step instructions on how many trials, how to read the period, or how to keep the orbit circular are given, and once ellipses appear the simulator is discarded and purely theoretical numbers are substituted without explanation of implementation. Reproduction would be difficult yet not impossible, placing it at the top of the 1-2 band.

Processing involves applying TâM^{-1/2} to generate ten data points and plotting a curve. The algebra is mostly correct, but the student mixes commas and decimal points, switches from simulation to theory abruptly, and performs no statistical checks beyond an RÂ² read-off by Desmos. These shortcomings constitute âsome inaccuracies or inconsistencies,â fitting band 1-2; the work is not so flawed as to merit a zero, so 2 is awarded.

Suggested improvements are limited to obtaining external data, incorporating Keplerâs laws, and adding another mass such as the Moon. They are realistic but stated in one sentence with no detail on implementation or expected effect, corresponding to ârealistic improvements are statedâ (band 1-2) at the lower score of 1.

Data (tables of solar-mass multipliers versus calculated periods) and a graph are presented in an organised way; headings, units (days, solar masses) and a fitted curve are visible. Some captions and axis labels are absent or partly cut in the screenshots, so precision is not perfect. Overall communication is clear enough but lacks full rigour, aligning with âeither clear or preciseâ in the 3-4 band.

The student briefly compares calculated periods to those of Mercury, Venus and Mars, noting approximate similarity when solar mass is 2.5 Mâ. This comparison is correct but superficial and lacks quantitative analysis or scholarly references, fulfilling only the âsuperficial comparisonâ descriptor in the 1-2 band with the higher score of 2.

The conclusion (larger solar mass â shorter year, smaller mass â longer year) matches the processed data and is explicitly linked to the graph. Nevertheless, because the analysis ignores elliptical orbits and uncertainties, the justification is not fully airtight. This corresponds to a conclusion that is âdescribed but not fully consistent,â placing it in band 3-4 at score 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is stated without contextual background. Adding a brief motivation or linking to real astronomical observations would improve clarity and relevance.
Stating the topic explicitly is helpful; consider expanding this section by summarizing why gravitational interaction is significant in modern astrophysics.
The derivation from Newtonâs law to $$T^2=\tfrac{4\pi^2r^3}{GM}$$ is clear and logically structured, demonstrating strong theoretical understanding.
Personal engagement (âme ha fascinadoâ) effectively communicates motivation and helps situate the research question within the studentâs interests.
Contextual discussion remains broad and textbook-level; it would benefit from referencing contemporary research or actual observational data to deepen specificity.
The unit for G is rendered as â\(\frac{Nm^2}{k\rho^2}\)â; correct the formatting and ensure consistency (e.g., \(\mathrm{m^2/kg^2}\)).
Intermediate calculation steps for the 0.5M and 2M cases are omitted, reducing transparency and reproducibility of results.
Include a worked example for one mass multiplier to illustrate calculation steps and enable others to follow the process precisely.
The expanded dataset from 0.5M to 5M solar masses strengthens the analysis by providing a more detailed trend assessment.
Tables present raw values but omit uncertainty estimates or error margins, which are essential for assessing data quality.
Add uncertainty calculations or propagate errors through the period formula and include error bars in tables and graph to reflect measurement precision.
Include the regression equation and RÂ² value directly on the graph to enhance clarity and support quantitative interpretation.
The comparison to planetary periods is superficial and lacks quantitative analysis of differences due to eccentricity or solar mass variations.
Present a comparative table of calculated periods versus actual planetary data (Mercury, Venus, Mars) to deepen the scientific context.
Evaluation section identifies specific limitations (simulator validation, omission of Keplerâs laws), demonstrating clear critical reflection.
Suggested improvements are listed but lack detail on implementation steps and expected impact on accuracy or validity.
For each proposed improvement (external data, Kepler laws, third mass), outline concrete steps, necessary resources, and anticipated precision gains.
Conclusion succinctly restates how solar mass variation influences Earthâs orbital period, directly addressing the research question.
Methodology description omits essential operational detailsânumber of trials, slider adjustments, measurement techniquesâhindering reproducibility.
Provide a detailed protocol: specify PhET slider values, number of repetitions per mass setting, timing method, and data recording format for full reproducibility.
The variables table effectively distinguishes dependent, independent, and controlled variables, aiding clarity of experimental design.
Justify using the arithmetic mean of perihelion and aphelion distances given the elliptical orbit; consider the effect of eccentricity on path length.
Verifying the simulatorâs 365-day result against the theoretical formula demonstrates careful validation of recorded data.
Decimal notation alternates between commas and periods. Use a single convention (e.g., periods for decimals) to avoid numerical misinterpretation.
Identifying that the simulator produces elliptical orbits at higher masses shows critical reflection on methodological limitations.
After recognizing orbital ellipticity, outline how Keplerâs laws would be applied in this context to maintain methodological accuracy.
Algebraic derivation of $$T' = \frac{T}{\sqrt{1.5}}$$ demonstrates solid understanding of the relationship between mass and orbital period.
Bibliographic entries lack access dates for online sources and do not follow a standardized citation format; revise per IB guidelines.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
